summary.name: Langali
key_uses: Expert Female Labor and Uterine Specialist

plant_profile.history: Unique History: Classified under the 'Upavisha' (semi-toxic) group in ancient Ayurvedic treatises like Charaka Samhita. The name 'Langali' literally translates to 'one with a plough-like root' (referring to its rhizome shape). Historically used as a bio-enhancer for various labor-assisting tonics. Mentioned in classical Sanskrit literature as the 'Flame Lily'. Physical Characteristics: A perennial, climbing herb with many nodes and tuberous rhizomes. The leaves are opposite, lanceolate, and glossy green. The flowers are attractive, solitary, and bright red-to-orange-yellow, arranged in terminal or axillary dense whorls. Found throughout India and tropical regions worldwide in gardens and wastelands of the sub-Himalayan tract and South India. Specific Chemical Compounds: Contains Colchicine, Gloriosine, and Superbine. The presence of colchicine (approx. 0.5-1.0% in rhizomes) is crucial for its neuro-relaxant, anti-tumor, and anti-tubercular action on the heart and skin cells. Strict Standardizations: The rhizome is standardized in API based on its greyish-white color and characteristic extremely bitter-pungent-biting taste. Physicochemical benchmarks: total ash (<10%), alcohol-soluble extractive (>5%). Purification Process (Shodhana): The raw rhizomes are traditionally soaked in 'Gomutra' (cow's urine) or 'Dugdha' (milk) for seven days followed by washing with hot water to remove excessive toxic alkaloids before medicinal use.

ayurvedic_properties.dosha_modulation: Powerful Vata and Kapha Hara (pacifies Vata and Kapha doshas). Due to its Katu (pungent) and Tikta (bitter) Rasa and Ushna (heating) Virya, it is the most prestigious herb for 'Shulaprashamana' (pain-relieving) and 'Vatavyadhi' (neurological disorders) therapies using its 'Vyavayi' (swift) and 'Tikshna' (sharp) potency. It may significantly aggravate Pitta if used in excess. It is an Upavisha (semi-poison).

medicinal_uses.expert_female_labor_and_uterine_speciali: Langali is the premier Ayurvedic remedy for all conditions of delayed labor (Mudha-garbha). Its 'Tikshna' (sharp) and 'Ushna' (heating) nature provided by colchicine alkaloids helps in stimulating uterine contractions and providing systemic stabilization of the 'Vata' channels (Srotas). WHO-aligned research recognizes its potent uterine stimulant and anti-inflammatory action when purified.
medicinal_uses.skin_disease_and_itch_relief__kandughna_: Its cooling and antimicrobial properties help in treating chronic skin conditions like acne, psoriasis, and eczema. The rhizome paste applied topically promotes rapid relief from acne and sun-burns while providing systemic 'Vranaropana' (wound-healing) effects.
medicinal_uses.worm_and_internal_parasite_care: Its extremely bitter-pungent nature makes it incomparable for managing stubborn intestinal parasites and worms (Krimighna) that thrive in a high-Kapha environment. It helps in cleared stagnant toxins from the gut.
medicinal_uses.inflammation_and_joint_care: Its systemic anti-inflammatory (Shothahara) properties help in reducing localized pain and stiffness in conditions like rheumatoid arthritis (Amavata). It promotes the excretion of metabolic waste and reducing joint inflammation.
medicinal_uses.cancer_and_immune_support: Traditionally used as 'Rasayana' (rejuvenative) for its anti-tumor and immune-modulating properties. It helps in protecting the body's 'Ojas' (vital essence) and promoting healthy cell regeneration by cleared metabolic toxins.
medicinal_uses.phytochemical_profile: Rich in Indole Alkaloids (Colchicine, Gloriosine), Tannins, Flavonoids, and diversos Triterpenes. Colchicine and Gloriosine are the primary bioactives responsible for its potent anti-inflammatory, anti-carcinogenic, and uterine-stimulant action on the heart and skin cells.

preparation_methods.purified_rhizome_powder__shuddha_: 125 mg to 250 mg (extremely low dose) of purified powder taken once daily with honey or warm water (for labor health) or warm milk (for general vitality).
preparation_methods.kasisadi_taila: Medicated oil preparation used primarily for local application on the pelvic region for labor health and joint pain.
preparation_methods.root_decoction__kwatha_: 20g of dried root boiled in 160ml water reduced to 40ml for chronic Vata issues. Take twice daily before meals.
preparation_methods.external_paste__lepa_: Ground fresh rhizome or powder applied directly to localized inflammations (Mumps, boils, skin rashes, hyperpigmentation) or onto the soles and palms for stimulating labor.

dosage.adults: Powder (Purified): 125-250 mg; Decoction: 20-50 ml; Taila: External Application, divided throughout the day before meals.
dosage.children: Strictly NOT recommended for children under any circumstances due to high toxicity.
dosage.elderly: Very low doses (30-60mg) are advisable initially, monitoring for any increase in dizziness or intestinal cramping.

contraindications: Strictly contraindicated in pregnancy (except for labor induction under expert care as it is abortifacient), during lactation, and in individuals with extreme high Pitta. Toxicity is extreme; non-purified use or overdose is lethal.
side_effects: Overdose or non-purified use results in muscle spasms, projectile vomiting, respiratory failure, and death. Discontinue if extreme purgation results in nausea.
drug_interactions: Monitor if taken alongside medications as it significantly increases the bioavailability of many drugs. Can potentiate synthetic anticoagulants.
safety.toxicity_notes: GRAS Status: NOT classified as GRAS. It is a potent semi-poison (Upavisha) and must only be used by qualified Ayurvedic physicians.
